one_line_takeaway: A decoupling framework (LLM4Vuln) plus a knowledge+context benchmark (UniVul) showing that external knowledge/context/CoT help traditional foundation models on logic-heavy Solidity but often hurt deep reasoning models — with a 14 zero-day / $3,576-bounty real-world validation.
key_finding: Whether external enhancement helps depends jointly on model TYPE and language. (1) Knowledge enhancement roughly DOUBLES F1 on average for traditional foundation models on Solidity (logic-heavy) but gives limited or negative gains on Java/C/C++ (where LLMs already know CWE-style patterns) — and for deep reasoning models it often HARMS precision/recall/F1 across all three languages, implying they already model vulnerability semantics internally. (2) Context supplementation gives only slight, inconsistent gains; foundation models often peak in F1 with context, deep reasoning models often peak without it. (3) CoT does not reliably raise recall/TP but consistently reduces false positives and raises true negatives. (4) A counter-intuitive effect: adding any external knowledge increases the number of NEGATIVE predictions across all languages, suggesting modern LLMs now reason about whether a vulnerability's preconditions actually hold rather than blindly trusting the injected hint. Real-world payoff: 14 confirmed zero-days and $3,576 in bounties.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is two-fold. (1) LLM4Vuln, a modular evaluation framework that decouples an LLM's *inherent* vulnerability-reasoning capability from four externally enhanceable factors formalized as R = f_L(T, K, C, P, I): vulnerability Knowledge retrieval (K), code Context supplementation (C), Prompt scheme (P), and Instruction-following (I). Each factor is a pluggable component, so the marginal effect of each enhancement can be measured against an isolated baseline. (2) UniVul, which the authors claim is the FIRST vulnerability benchmark providing both retrievable vulnerability knowledge AND context-supplementable code, spanning three languages (Solidity, Java, C/C++). Note: prior LLM-vuln detectors (GPTScan, GPTLens, TitanFuzz, FuzzGPT, etc.) and prior benchmarks (PrimeVul, MegaVul) are cited as Related Work / motivation — they are NOT this paper's contribution; the authors explicitly state PrimeVul and MegaVul are incompatible with their framework because they lack retrievable knowledge and surrounding context.
threat_model: Defender-side / evaluation paper (not an attack paper). The security concern is exploitable software vulnerabilities — business-logic bugs in Solidity smart contracts (e.g., missing duplicate-entry checks, decimal-precision miscalculations, imbalanced liquidity provision that can cause fund loss) and language-level bugs in C/C++/Java (memory safety, unsafe deserialization, command/SQL injection, XSS). The paper studies how well off-the-shelf LLMs can *reason about and detect* such vulnerabilities as an auditing aid, and whether that ability comes from the model itself vs. external retrieval/context/prompting. No adversary against the LLM itself is modeled; this is an assessment of LLMs as vulnerability-detection tools, culminating in a real bug-bounty deployment.
limitations: (1) Benchmark scale is modest: only 147 vulnerable + 147 non-vulnerable functions; the large 3,528/8,232 figures come from configuration cross-products, not distinct code. (2) Java/C/C++ knowledge bases are SYNTHETIC — GPT-4.1 generated 10 code examples + audit-style reports per CWE (770 Java, 860 C/C++ items), so knowledge quality/realism is model-dependent. (3) Retrieval quality is imperfect and unevenly measured: only 68/100 sampled Solidity cases and 36/100 Java cases had knowledge aligned with ground truth; low-level bugs (integer overflow) and procedural bugs (SSRF, CSRF) are underrepresented. (4) The evaluation pipeline is heavily GPT-4.1-dependent (sanitization, summarization, structuring, annotation), risking circularity — though type-matching annotation accuracy was 81% Solidity / 98% Java / 97% C/C++ vs. 100% binary. (5) The zero-day pilot is Solidity-only and small (29 submissions), with a manual filtering step before submission, so the end-to-end automation claim is partial. (6) Uses a specific 2025 model snapshot (GPT-4.1, o4-mini, etc.); results may not transfer to newer models.

datasets_used: UniVul (this paper's own benchmark), 3 languages, 147 vulnerable + 147 non-vulnerable = 294 code cases total
Solidity: 1,013-item knowledge set from Code4Rena (251 projects, Jan 2021–Jul 2023) + testing set of 51 vulnerable / 51 non-vulnerable functions from projects audited after Jul 2023
Java: 770-item synthetic knowledge set (77 CWEs × 10 GPT-4.1-generated examples) + testing set of 46 vulnerable / 46 non-vulnerable functions from CVE/BigVul (Jan 2013–Dec 2022)
C/C++: 860-item synthetic knowledge set (86 CWEs × 10 examples) + testing set of 50 vulnerable / 50 non-vulnerable functions from CVE/BigVul (Jan 2013–Dec 2022)
Four real bug-bounty Solidity projects for the pilot: Apebond, Glyph AMM, StakeStone, Hajime (confirmed via Secure3)
Cited-but-not-used prior benchmarks: PrimeVul and MegaVul (stated incompatible, so NOT used as baselines)
quantitative_results: 3,528 evaluation scenarios (294 × 3 knowledge × 2 context × 2 prompt) and 8,232 test cases (294 × 7 knowledge retrievals × 2 × 2)
Solidity F1 for traditional foundation models nearly doubles on average with knowledge enhancement
GPT-4.1 + CoT on Solidity: recall DROPS from 33.33% to 19.61% (original reports) and 11.76% (summarized knowledge); in another combination from 17.65% to 6.54% / 7.84%
Java/C/C++: TP count decreases when any knowledge is added in 89 of 96 combinations
Context supplementation on C/C++: TPs increase in 28/36 and precision improves in 29/36 combinations (most consistent language); Solidity precision improves in 22/36
GPT-4.1 auto-annotation accuracy: 100% for binary vulnerable/not; vulnerability-TYPE match accuracy 81% (Solidity), 98% (Java), 97% (C/C++)
Retrieval quality: knowledge aligned with ground truth in 68/100 sampled Solidity positives, only 36/100 Java positives

Zero-day pilot: 29 issues submitted, 14 confirmed, $3,576 total bounty — Apebond $376 (4/12), Glyph AMM $329 (4/6), StakeStone $2,281 (5/9), Hajime $590 (1/2); 3 of 4 case-study bugs were logic bugs undetectable by static analysis
baselines_compared: Six LLMs compared against each other: traditional foundation models GPT-4.1, Phi-3-mini-128k, Llama-3-8B vs. deep reasoning models o4-mini, DeepSeek-R1, QwQ-32B
Enhancement arms compared to the no-enhancement baseline f_L(T): no-knowledge (Nk) vs. original reports (Ok) vs. summarized knowledge (Sk)
Cross-cutting comparison of model TYPE (traditional foundation vs. deep reasoning) as the primary analytical axis
